when_to_use: When handling transaction and problem-solving review.
When speaking with locals in Taiwan.
When turning known words into spoken responses.
when_not_to_use: Do not translate word-by-word from English.
Do not use with vocabulary you have not learned yet.
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan Mandarin favors clear, polite, compact sentences.
word_order_notes: Time expressions usually come before the subject in Mandarin.
english_contrast: English word order differs; Mandarin places context first.
korean_contrast: Korean learners may overuse sentence-final politeness markers.

drill_examples.text: 我去了。

drill_examples.text: 你喜歡了嗎？
drill_examples.pinyin: nǐ xǐhuan le ma？
drill_examples.translation_en: Did you like?
drill_examples.translation_ko: 좋아하다했습니까?

drill_examples.text: 他想水。
drill_examples.pinyin: tā xiǎng 水。
drill_examples.translation_en: He wants water.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 그는 물을/를 원하다합니다.

drill_examples.text: 我喜歡過人。
drill_examples.pinyin: wǒ xǐhuan guò 人。
drill_examples.translation_en: I have liked person before.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 저는 사람을/를 좋아하다한 적이 있습니다.

explanation_en: Use '了' at the end of a sentence to indicate that an action has been completed. This is the basic past tense marker in Chinese.
title: Completed Action with 了
meaning: Indicates an action is completed
when_to_use: When talking about something you finished doing
When reporting what happened
When answering 'what did you do?'
when_not_to_use: When describing habitual actions
When talking about future plans
When using negated past actions (use 没有 instead)
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 了 is commonly used in casual conversation to confirm completion. It's less formal than using 已經 (already).
word_order_notes: 了 always comes at the end of the sentence, after the object. Do not place it between the verb and object.
english_contrast: English uses past tense verb forms (e.g., 'ate', 'drank'), while Chinese adds 了 at the end of the sentence.
korean_contrast: Korean uses '-었/았다' attached to the verb stem, while Chinese uses 了 at the end of the sentence.

pattern: 昨天 / 上個禮拜 / 上個月 + Subject + Verb + Object + 了
explanation_en: Use a past time word at the beginning of the sentence to specify when the action happened. Common time words include 昨天 (yesterday), 上個禮拜 (last week), 上個月 (last month), 剛才 (just now).
title: Past Time Words with 了
meaning: Specifies when a past action occurred
when_to_use: When you want to specify the time of a past action
When telling a story about yesterday or last week
When answering 'when did you do that?'
when_not_to_use: When the time is obvious from context
When talking about future events
When using 今天 (today) for actions not yet completed
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 昨天 is very common in daily conversation. You can also use 上個禮拜 for last week or 上個月 for last month.
word_order_notes: Time word comes first in the sentence, before the subject. This is different from English where time can come at the end.
english_contrast: English can place time words at the beginning or end (e.g., 'I ate breakfast yesterday' or 'Yesterday I ate breakfast'). Chinese requires time words at the beginning.
korean_contrast: Korean places time words before the verb but after the subject (e.g., '나는 어제 아침을 먹었다'), while Chinese places time words at the very beginning.

pattern: Subject + Verb + Object + 了，然後 + Subject + Verb + Object + 了
explanation_en: Use 然後 (then) to connect two completed actions in sequence. Both actions should end with 了 to indicate they are completed.
title: Sequencing Past Actions with 然後
meaning: Describes a sequence of past events
when_to_use: When telling a story about what you did in order
When describing your daily routine yesterday
When explaining a series of past actions
when_not_to_use: When actions happened at the same time
When describing future plans
When using 先 (first) and 再 (then) for future actions
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 然後 is very common in spoken Chinese to connect events. It's similar to 'and then' in English.
word_order_notes: Each clause follows the Subject + Verb + Object + 了 pattern. 然後 connects the two clauses.
english_contrast: English uses 'and then' or 'then' to connect past actions. Chinese uses 然後 between two clauses, each ending with 了.
korean_contrast: Korean uses '-고' or '-서' to connect sequential actions (e.g., '먹고 갔다'), while Chinese uses 然後 between separate clauses.

negative_examples.text: 我吃早餐了，然後我喝咖啡了。
negative_examples.error: This sentence is correct. No error.

incorrect_examples.text: 我吃早餐了，然後喝咖啡了。
incorrect_examples.error: Missing subject '我' before '喝咖啡了' in the second clause.

incorrect_examples.text: 我吃早餐，然後我喝咖啡了。
incorrect_examples.error: First action '吃早餐' is missing 了 to indicate completion.

transformation_drills: Combine '我起床了' and '我刷牙了' using 然後.
Combine '我看電視了' and '我睡覺了' using 然後.
production_drills: Tell your partner what you did after you got home yesterday.
Tell your partner your morning routine yesterday using 然後.
common_error_patterns: Omitting the subject in the second clause
Forgetting 了 in one of the clauses

repair_feedback.word_order: Each clause must have its own subject and end with 了.
repair_feedback.politeness: This structure is neutral in politeness.
repair_feedback.vocabulary: Use action verbs like 起床, 刷牙, 吃, 喝, 看, 去 with this pattern.

explanation_en: Use 然後 to connect two past events in chronological order, meaning 'and then' or 'after that'.
title: Sequencing with 然後
meaning: Connecting two events in order
when_to_use: When telling a story about what happened step by step
When describing your daily routine or a past sequence
when_not_to_use: When events happen at the same time
When you want to show cause and effect (use 因為...所以)
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 然後 is very common in spoken narratives. You can use it multiple times in a row to keep the story going.
word_order_notes: 然後 always comes between the two events. The first event is stated, then 然後, then the second event.
english_contrast: English uses 'and then' or 'after that'. Chinese uses 然後 in the same position between clauses.
korean_contrast: Korean uses 그리고 (geurigo) or 그 다음에 (geu daeume) in a similar way, placed between clauses.

correct_examples.text: 我昨天早上起床，然後吃早餐。

correct_examples.translation_en: I got up yesterday morning and then ate breakfast.

drill_examples.translation_ko: 그는 먼저 책을 보고, 그다음에 음악을 들어요.

negative_examples.error: 然後 needs a second event after it; it cannot be at the end of a sentence.

incorrect_examples.text: 我昨天去公園，然後我昨天去商店。

repair_feedback.word_order: Put 然後 between the two events, not at the end.
repair_feedback.politeness: This structure is neutral; no special politeness needed.
repair_feedback.vocabulary: Use simple past events you know, like 吃早餐, 去公司, 看書.

pattern: 先 A，然後 B，再 C
explanation_en: Use 先...然後...再... to list three or more steps in order, meaning 'first A, then B, then C'.
title: Multiple Steps with 先...然後...再...
meaning: Listing a sequence of actions
when_to_use: When describing a process with three or more steps
When telling a story with a clear beginning, middle, and end
when_not_to_use: When there are only two steps (use just 然後)
When the order is not important
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, this pattern is very common for giving instructions or telling a story. You can add more steps by repeating 然後 or 再.
word_order_notes: 先 comes before the first action, 然後 before the second, 再 before the third. Do not put 先 after the first action.
english_contrast: English uses 'first...then...then...' or 'first...after that...finally'. Chinese uses 先...然後...再... in the same order.
korean_contrast: Korean uses 먼저...그리고...그 다음에... (meonjeo...geurigo...geu daeume...) in a similar pattern.

correct_examples.text: 我昨天先起床，然後刷牙洗臉，再吃早餐。

correct_examples.translation_en: We first found the restaurant, then ordered food, and then waited a bit.

negative_examples.text: 我先吃早餐，然後去公司，然後看書。
negative_examples.error: Using 然後 twice is okay but less natural; use 再 for the third step.

incorrect_examples.text: 先我吃早餐，然後我去公司，再看書。
incorrect_examples.error: 先 must come right before the verb, not before the subject. Say '我先吃早餐'.

transformation_drills: Change '我起床。我刷牙。我吃早餐。' into one sentence using 先...然後...再...
production_drills: Describe your morning routine using 先...然後...再...
common_error_patterns: Putting 先 after the subject instead of before the verb
Using 然後 for all steps instead of 再

repair_feedback.word_order: 先 goes right before the first verb, not before the subject.
repair_feedback.politeness: This pattern is neutral; fine for both casual and polite speech.
repair_feedback.vocabulary: Use daily actions like 起床, 刷牙, 吃早餐, 去公司.

explanation_en: Place 了 after the verb to indicate a completed past action. This is a review of the basic past tense structure.
title: Past Action with 了 (Review)
meaning: Indicating a completed action
when_to_use: When talking about something that happened and finished in the past
When answering 'what did you do?' questions
when_not_to_use: When describing habitual actions (use present tense)
When the action is ongoing or not yet completed
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 了 is very common in spoken Chinese for past events. However, for verbs like 是, 有, 在, 了 is not used.
word_order_notes: 了 goes immediately after the verb, before the object. Do not put 了 at the end of the sentence if there is an object.
english_contrast: English uses past tense (e.g., 'ate'). Chinese uses 了 after the verb. No change in verb form.
korean_contrast: Korean uses -었/았다 (-eot/atda) at the end of the verb. Chinese places 了 after the verb but before the object.

correct_examples.text: 我昨天買了一本書，然後看完了。
correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒ zuótiān mǎi le yī běn shū, ránhòu kàn wán le.
correct_examples.translation_en: I bought a book yesterday and then finished reading it.

correct_examples.text: 他早上起床，先刷牙洗臉，然後吃早餐。
correct_examples.pinyin: Tā zǎoshang qǐchuáng, xiān shuāyá xǐliǎn, ránhòu chī zǎocān.
correct_examples.translation_en: He got up in the morning, brushed his teeth and washed his face first, then ate breakfast.

correct_examples.text: 我們去了公園，在那裡散步，然後回家。
correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒmen qù le gōngyuán, zài nàlǐ sànbù, ránhòu huí jiā.
correct_examples.translation_en: We went to the park, took a walk there, and then went home.

correct_examples.text: 她先喝了咖啡，然後開始做功課。
correct_examples.pinyin: Tā xiān hē le kāfēi, ránhòu kāishǐ zuò gōngkè.
correct_examples.translation_en: She drank coffee first, then started doing homework.

correct_examples.text: 我昨天去超市買了菜和肉，然後回家煮飯。
correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒ zuótiān qù chāoshì mǎi le cài hé ròu, ránhòu huí jiā zhǔ fàn.
correct_examples.translation_en: I went to the supermarket yesterday, bought vegetables and meat, then went home and cooked.

correct_examples.text: 他先去了銀行，然後去圖書館看書。
correct_examples.pinyin: Tā xiān qù le yínháng, ránhòu qù túshūguǎn kàn shū.
correct_examples.translation_en: He went to the bank first, then went to the library to read.

correct_examples.text: 我們先吃了午餐，然後去逛街。
correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒmen xiān chī le wǔcān, ránhòu qù guàngjiē.
correct_examples.translation_en: We ate lunch first, then went shopping.

correct_examples.text: 她昨天買了一件衣服，可是太大了，所以今天去換。
correct_examples.pinyin: Tā zuótiān mǎi le yī jiàn yīfu, kěshì tài dà le, suǒyǐ jīntiān qù huàn.
correct_examples.translation_en: She bought a piece of clothing yesterday, but it was too big, so she went to exchange it today.

drill_examples.text: 我昨天買了一本書，然後看完了。
drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ zuótiān mǎi le yī běn shū, ránhòu kàn wán le.
drill_examples.translation_en: I bought a book yesterday and then finished reading it.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 저는 어제 책 한 권을 샀고, 그다음에 다 읽었어요.

drill_examples.text: 他早上起床，先刷牙洗臉，然後吃早餐。
drill_examples.pinyin: Tā zǎoshang qǐchuáng, xiān shuāyá xǐliǎn, ránhòu chī zǎocān.

drill_examples.translation_ko: 그는 아침에 일어나서, 먼저 이를 닦고 세수한 다음, 아침을 먹었어요.

drill_examples.text: 我們去了公園，在那裡散步，然後回家。
drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒmen qù le gōngyuán, zài nàlǐ sànbù, ránhòu huí jiā.
drill_examples.translation_en: We went to the park, took a walk there, and then went home.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 우리는 공원에 가서 거기서 산책하고, 그다음에 집에 돌아왔어요.

drill_examples.text: 她先喝了咖啡，然後開始做功課。
drill_examples.pinyin: Tā xiān hē le kāfēi, ránhòu kāishǐ zuò gōngkè.

drill_examples.text: 我昨天去超市買了菜和肉，然後回家煮飯。
drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ zuótiān qù chāoshì mǎi le cài hé ròu, ránhòu huí jiā zhǔ fàn.
drill_examples.translation_en: I went to the supermarket yesterday, bought vegetables and meat, then went home and cooked.

drill_examples.text: 他先去了銀行，然後去圖書館看書。
drill_examples.pinyin: Tā xiān qù le yínháng, ránhòu qù túshūguǎn kàn shū.

drill_examples.translation_ko: 그는 먼저 은행에 갔고, 그다음에 도서관에 가서 책을 읽었어요.

drill_examples.text: 我們先吃了午餐，然後去逛街。
drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒmen xiān chī le wǔcān, ránhòu qù guàngjiē.
drill_examples.translation_en: We ate lunch first, then went shopping.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 우리는 먼저 점심을 먹고, 그다음에 쇼핑하러 갔어요.

drill_examples.text: 她昨天買了一件衣服，可是太大了，所以今天去換。
drill_examples.pinyin: Tā zuótiān mǎi le yī jiàn yīfu, kěshì tài dà le, suǒyǐ jīntiān qù huàn.
drill_examples.translation_en: She bought a piece of clothing yesterday, but it was too big, so she went to exchange it today.

negative_examples.error: Time words like 昨天 should come before the verb, not after the object.

incorrect_examples.error: This is correct if there is no object, but with an object like 早餐, 了 should go after the verb: '我吃了早餐'.

transformation_drills: Change '我吃早餐' into past tense using 了.
production_drills: Tell me one thing you did this morning using 了.
common_error_patterns: Putting 了 at the end of the sentence instead of after the verb
Using 了 with 是 or 有

repair_feedback.word_order: 了 goes right after the verb, before the object.
repair_feedback.politeness: This is neutral; no special politeness needed.
repair_feedback.vocabulary: Use simple verbs like 吃, 喝, 看, 去, 買.

explanation_en: Use 過 (guò) after a verb to indicate that someone has experienced or done something at least once in the past. It emphasizes the experience itself, not when it happened.
title: Expressing Past Experience with 過
meaning: Have done something before
when_to_use: When asking if someone has ever done something
When sharing personal experiences or travel stories
When talking about things you have tried or visited
when_not_to_use: Do not use 過 for actions that happened at a specific time (use 了 instead)
Do not use 過 for ongoing or habitual actions
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 過 is very common in casual conversation, especially when talking about travel (去過台北), food (吃過滷肉飯), or experiences (看過那部電影). It's neutral and polite.
word_order_notes: Place 過 directly after the verb and before the object. The word order is: Subject + Verb + 過 + Object.
english_contrast: In English, we use 'have/has + past participle' (e.g., 'I have been to Taipei'). In Chinese, 過 replaces the auxiliary verb 'have' and is attached to the main verb.
korean_contrast: In Korean, the equivalent is '-아/어 본 적이 있다' (e.g., '저는 타이베이에 가 본 적이 있어요'). 過 is simpler and directly attached to the verb.

correct_examples.text: 你去過臺北嗎？
correct_examples.pinyin: Nǐ qù guò Táiběi ma?
correct_examples.translation_en: Have you been to Taipei?

correct_examples.text: 我吃過滷肉飯，很好吃。
correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒ chī guò lǔròufàn, hěn hǎochī.
correct_examples.translation_en: I have eaten braised pork rice; it's very delicious.

correct_examples.text: 他從來沒有喝過珍珠奶茶。
correct_examples.pinyin: Tā cónglái méiyǒu hē guò zhēnzhū nǎichá.
correct_examples.translation_en: He has never drunk bubble tea.

correct_examples.text: 我們看過九份的風景，很漂亮。
correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒmen kàn guò Jiǔfèn de fēngjǐng, hěn piàoliang.
correct_examples.translation_en: We have seen the scenery of Jiufen; it's very beautiful.

correct_examples.text: 你聽過這首歌嗎？
correct_examples.pinyin: Nǐ tīng guò zhè shǒu gē ma?
correct_examples.translation_en: Have you heard this song?

correct_examples.text: 我學過中文，可是不太流利。
correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒ xué guò Zhōngwén, kěshì bú tài liúlì.
correct_examples.translation_en: I have studied Chinese, but I'm not very fluent.

correct_examples.text: 她做過臺灣小吃，很好吃。
correct_examples.pinyin: Tā zuò guò Táiwān xiǎochī, hěn hǎochī.
correct_examples.translation_en: She has made Taiwanese snacks; they are very delicious.

correct_examples.text: 這是我們第一次去海邊玩。
correct_examples.pinyin: Zhè shì wǒmen dì yī cì qù hǎibiān wán.
correct_examples.translation_en: This is our first time going to the beach to have fun.

drill_examples.text: 你去過臺北嗎？

drill_examples.text: 我吃過滷肉飯，很好吃。

drill_examples.text: 他從來沒有喝過珍珠奶茶。

drill_examples.text: 我們看過九份的風景，很漂亮。
drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒmen kàn guò Jiǔfèn de fēngjǐng, hěn piàoliang.

drill_examples.text: 你聽過這首歌嗎？

drill_examples.text: 她做過臺灣小吃，很好吃。

negative_examples.error: This sentence uses 過 with a specific time (昨天), which is incorrect. 過 is for general experience, not specific past events. Use 了 instead: 我昨天去了台北。
過 cannot be used with frequency adverbs like 常常 (often). Use the simple verb instead: 我常常吃滷肉飯。

incorrect_examples.error: The word order is wrong. 過 must come after the verb, not before. Correct: 我看過電影。
Double 過 is incorrect. Use 沒有 + verb + 過: 他沒有去過台北。

transformation_drills: Change '我吃滷肉飯' to express experience: 我吃過滷肉飯。
Change '他看那部電影' to a question about experience: 他看過那部電影嗎？
production_drills: Ask your partner: 你去過台灣哪裡？
Tell your partner: 我吃過珍珠奶茶。
common_error_patterns: Using 過 with specific time words like 昨天 or 上個星期
Placing 過 before the verb instead of after

repair_feedback.word_order: Remember: 過 always comes right after the verb, like 吃過, 去過, 看過.
repair_feedback.politeness: Using 過 is polite and natural in Taiwan. No special politeness adjustment needed.
repair_feedback.vocabulary: Use 過 with action verbs like 去, 吃, 喝, 看, 聽, 學, 做.

pattern: Subject + 沒有 + Verb + 過 + Object
explanation_en: Use 沒有 + verb + 過 to express that someone has never done something before. This is the negative form of the experience 過 construction.
title: Negating Past Experience with 沒有...過
meaning: Have never done something before
when_to_use: When saying you have never done something
When answering 'no' to a 過 question
When expressing lack of experience
when_not_to_use: Do not use 不 instead of 沒有 for this pattern
Do not use 過 with 了 in the same sentence
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 從來沒有 + verb + 過 is a stronger way to say 'never ever'. For example: 我從來沒有去過花蓮。This is common in casual speech.
word_order_notes: Place 沒有 before the verb, and 過 after the verb. The word order is: Subject + 沒有 + Verb + 過 + Object.
english_contrast: In English, we say 'I have never been to Taipei.' In Chinese, 沒有 negates the experience, and 過 marks the verb. The structure is different: 'I not have go over Taipei.'
korean_contrast: In Korean, the equivalent is '-아/어 본 적이 없다' (e.g., '저는 타이베이에 가 본 적이 없어요'). 沒有...過 is more direct and common in spoken Chinese.

correct_examples.text: 我沒有去過臺北的故宮。
correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒ méiyǒu qù guò Táiběi de Gùgōng.
correct_examples.translation_en: I have never been to the National Palace Museum in Taipei.

correct_examples.text: 你吃過臺南的牛肉麵嗎？
correct_examples.pinyin: Nǐ chī guò Táinán de niúròumiàn ma?
correct_examples.translation_en: Have you ever eaten beef noodles in Tainan?

correct_examples.text: 我從來沒有喝過珍珠奶茶。
correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒ cónglái méiyǒu hē guò zhēnzhū nǎichá.
correct_examples.translation_en: I have never had bubble tea before.

correct_examples.text: 他沒有看過這部電影。
correct_examples.pinyin: Tā méiyǒu kàn guò zhè bù diànyǐng.
correct_examples.translation_en: He has never seen this movie.

correct_examples.text: 我們沒有學過這個句子。
correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒmen méiyǒu xué guò zhège jùzi.
correct_examples.translation_en: We have never studied this sentence before.

correct_examples.text: 你聽過這首歌嗎？我沒有聽過。
correct_examples.pinyin: Nǐ tīng guò zhè shǒu gē ma? Wǒ méiyǒu tīng guò.
correct_examples.translation_en: Have you heard this song? I haven't heard it.

correct_examples.text: 她沒有去過國外旅行。
correct_examples.pinyin: Tā méiyǒu qù guò guówài lǚxíng.
correct_examples.translation_en: She has never traveled abroad.

correct_examples.text: 我沒有吃過滷肉飯，但是我想試試看。
correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒ méiyǒu chī guò lǔròufàn, dànshì wǒ xiǎng shì shì kàn.
correct_examples.translation_en: I have never eaten braised pork rice, but I want to try it.

drill_examples.text: 我從來沒有喝過珍珠奶茶。

drill_examples.translation_en: He has never seen this movie.

drill_examples.text: 我們沒有學過這個句子。

drill_examples.translation_en: We have never studied this sentence before.

drill_examples.text: 我沒有吃過滷肉飯，但是我想試試看。
drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ méiyǒu chī guò lǔròufàn, dànshì wǒ xiǎng shì shì kàn.

drill_examples.translation_ko: 저는 루로우판을 먹어 본 적이 없지만, 한번 먹어 보고 싶습니다.

negative_examples.error: Use 沒有 instead of 不 for negating experience. Correct: 我沒有去過台北。
Do not use 了 with 沒有...過. 了 indicates completion, not experience. Correct: 我沒有去過台北。

incorrect_examples.error: 過 must come after the verb, not before. Correct: 我沒有吃過滷肉飯。

incorrect_examples.text: 他沒有看過過那部電影。
incorrect_examples.error: Double 過 is incorrect. Use only one 過 after the verb. Correct: 他沒有看過那部電影。

transformation_drills: Change '我去過台北' to negative: 我沒有去過台北。
Change '他吃過滷肉飯' to a negative statement: 他沒有吃過滷肉飯。
production_drills: Tell your partner something you have never done: 我沒有去過墾丁。
Ask your partner: 你有沒有吃過臭豆腐？
common_error_patterns: Using 不 instead of 沒有
Placing 過 before the verb in negative sentences

repair_feedback.word_order: Remember: 沒有 + verb + 過. For example: 沒有去過, 沒有吃過.
repair_feedback.politeness: This pattern is neutral and polite. No special adjustment needed.
repair_feedback.vocabulary: Use 沒有...過 with the same verbs as 過: 去, 吃, 喝, 看, 聽, 學, 做.

pattern: 以前 + Subject + Verb + 過 + Object
explanation_en: Use 以前 (yǐqián) at the beginning of a sentence to indicate that something happened in the past, often combined with 過 to emphasize that it was an experience. 以前 means 'before' or 'in the past'.
title: Talking About Past Experiences with 以前
meaning: In the past, I have done something
when_to_use: When introducing a past experience without a specific time
When comparing past and present experiences
When telling stories about what you did before
when_not_to_use: Do not use 以前 with specific time words like 昨天 or 上個星期 (use those alone)
Do not use 以前 for future plans
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 以前 is very common in storytelling and casual conversation. It can be used with or without 過. For example: 以前我去過台北。or 以前我住在台北。Both are natural.
word_order_notes: 以前 usually comes at the beginning of the sentence, before the subject. The word order is: 以前 + Subject + Verb + 過 + Object.
english_contrast: In English, we say 'Before, I have been to Taipei.' In Chinese, 以前 sets the time frame, and 過 marks the experience. The structure is similar but 以前 is more flexible.
korean_contrast: In Korean, the equivalent is '예전에' or '전에' (e.g., '예전에 저는 타이베이에 가 본 적이 있어요'). 以前 is used similarly at the beginning of the sentence.

correct_examples.text: 我以前去過臺北的故宮。
correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒ yǐqián qù guò Táiběi de Gùgōng.
correct_examples.translation_en: I have been to the National Palace Museum in Taipei before.

correct_examples.text: 你以前吃過臺灣的小吃嗎？
correct_examples.pinyin: Nǐ yǐqián chī guò Táiwān de xiǎochī ma?
correct_examples.translation_en: Have you ever eaten Taiwanese street food before?

correct_examples.text: 我從來沒有去過國外旅行。
correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒ cónglái méiyǒu qù guò guówài lǚxíng.
correct_examples.translation_en: I have never traveled abroad before.

correct_examples.text: 他以前在臺中吃過很好吃的牛肉麵。
correct_examples.pinyin: Tā yǐqián zài Táizhōng chī guò hěn hǎochī de niúròumiàn.
correct_examples.translation_en: He has eaten very delicious beef noodles in Taichung before.

correct_examples.text: 我們以前去過墾丁的海邊，風景很漂亮。

correct_examples.translation_en: We have been to the beach in Kenting before, and the scenery was very beautiful.

correct_examples.text: 你以前喝過珍珠奶茶嗎？
correct_examples.pinyin: Nǐ yǐqián hē guò zhēnzhū nǎichá ma?
correct_examples.translation_en: Have you ever drunk bubble tea before?

correct_examples.text: 她以前學過中文，所以會說一點。
correct_examples.pinyin: Tā yǐqián xué guò Zhōngwén, suǒyǐ huì shuō yīdiǎn.
correct_examples.translation_en: She has studied Chinese before, so she can speak a little.

drill_examples.translation_en: Have you ever eaten Taiwanese street food before?
drill_examples.translation_ko: 예전에 대만의 길거리 음식을 먹어 본 적이 있나요?

drill_examples.text: 我從來沒有去過國外旅行。
drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ cónglái méiyǒu qù guò guówài lǚxíng.
drill_examples.translation_en: I have never traveled abroad before.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 저는 예전에 해외 여행을 가 본 적이 전혀 없습니다.

drill_examples.text: 他以前在臺中吃過很好吃的牛肉麵。

drill_examples.translation_en: He has eaten very delicious beef noodles in Taichung before.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 그는 예전에 타이중에서 아주 맛있는 소고기 국수를 먹어 본 적이 있습니다.

drill_examples.text: 我們以前去過墾丁的海邊，風景很漂亮。
drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒmen yǐqián qù guò Kěndīng de hǎibiān, fēngjǐng hěn piàoliang.
drill_examples.translation_en: We have been to the beach in Kenting before, and the scenery was very beautiful.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 우리는 예전에 컨딩의 해변에 가 본 적이 있는데, 경치가 아주 아름다웠습니다.

drill_examples.text: 你以前喝過珍珠奶茶嗎？
drill_examples.pinyin: Nǐ yǐqián hē guò zhēnzhū nǎichá ma?
drill_examples.translation_en: Have you ever drunk bubble tea before?
drill_examples.translation_ko: 예전에 버블티를 마셔 본 적이 있나요?

drill_examples.text: 我聽過這首歌，可是沒有聽過這個版本。
drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ tīng guò zhè shǒu gē, kěshì méiyǒu tīng guò zhège bǎnběn.
drill_examples.translation_en: I have heard this song before, but I haven't heard this version.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 저는 이 노래를 들어 본 적이 있지만, 이 버전은 들어 본 적이 없습니다.

drill_examples.text: 她以前學過中文，所以會說一點。
drill_examples.pinyin: Tā yǐqián xué guò Zhōngwén, suǒyǐ huì shuō yīdiǎn.
drill_examples.translation_en: She has studied Chinese before, so she can speak a little.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 그녀는 예전에 중국어를 배운 적이 있어서 조금 말할 수 있습니다.

negative_examples.error: Do not use 以前 with a specific time like 昨天. Use either 以前 or 昨天, not both. Correct: 我昨天去過台北。or 以前我去過台北。
This sentence is actually correct! But note: 以前 can be used with negative 沒有...過 to mean 'I have never been to Taipei before.'

incorrect_examples.error: Do not use 了 with 過 when 以前 is used. 了 indicates completion, which conflicts with the general experience meaning. Correct: 我以前去過台北。
Double 過 is incorrect. Use only one 過 after the verb. Correct: 以前我去過台北。

transformation_drills: Add 以前 to '我去過台北': 以前我去過台北。
Change '以前我吃過滷肉飯' to a negative sentence: 以前我沒有吃過滷肉飯。
production_drills: Tell your partner: 以前我去過淡水。
Ask your partner: 以前你有沒有去過九份？
common_error_patterns: Using 以前 with specific time words
Using 了 with 過 in the same sentence

repair_feedback.word_order: Place 以前 at the beginning of the sentence, before the subject.
repair_feedback.politeness: Using 以前 is neutral and polite. No special adjustment needed.
repair_feedback.vocabulary: Use 以前 with 過 verbs like 去, 吃, 喝, 看, 聽, 學, 做.

explanation_en: Use '正在' to emphasize that an action is in progress right now. It's like saying 'in the middle of doing something'.
title: Emphasizing Ongoing Action with 正在
meaning: Indicates an action is actively happening at this moment.
when_to_use: When you want to stress that someone is currently doing something
When answering 'what are you doing?' with focus on the ongoing nature
when_not_to_use: For habitual actions (use 每天/常常 instead)
For future plans (use 要/打算 instead)
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 正在 is common in both spoken and written Chinese. It adds a sense of 'right in the middle of' and is slightly more formal than just 在.
word_order_notes: 正在 comes directly after the subject and before the verb. No time words can interrupt this structure.
english_contrast: English uses 'am/is/are -ing' for ongoing actions. 正在 is similar but more emphatic than just 在.
korean_contrast: Korean uses '~고 있다' for ongoing actions. 正在 is like '~고 있는 중이다' (in the middle of doing).

correct_examples.translation_en: I am reading a book.

correct_examples.text: 她正在滑手機。
correct_examples.pinyin: Tā zhèngzài huá shǒujī.
correct_examples.translation_en: She is scrolling on her phone.

correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒmen zhèngzài kāihuì.

drill_examples.translation_en: I am reading a book.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 저는 책을 보고 있어요.

drill_examples.text: 你正在寫功課嗎？
drill_examples.pinyin: Nǐ zhèngzài xiě gōngkè ma?

drill_examples.text: 他正在講電話。
drill_examples.pinyin: Tā zhèngzài jiǎng diànhuà.

drill_examples.text: 我媽媽正在煮飯。

negative_examples.error: 正在 must come before the verb, not after. Correct: 我正在吃飯。
正在 is only for actions, not states like '是'. Use 他現在是學生。

incorrect_examples.error: 正在 is for right now, not habitual actions. Use 我每天看書。
正在 cannot be used for future. Use 他明天要去台北。

transformation_drills: Change '他看書' to emphasize ongoing action: 他正在看書。
Change '我吃飯' to emphasize right now: 我正在吃飯。
production_drills: Answer: 你現在做什麼？(Use 正在)
Describe what your friend is doing right now using 正在
common_error_patterns: Putting 正在 after the verb
Using 正在 with stative verbs like 是, 有, 喜歡

repair_feedback.word_order: Remember: Subject + 正在 + Verb + Object. 正在 always before the verb.
repair_feedback.politeness: Using 正在 is neutral; for polite interruption, add 不好意思 before asking.
repair_feedback.vocabulary: Use action verbs with 正在, not state verbs.

explanation_en: Use '在' to indicate an action is in progress. It's the most common way to express ongoing actions in daily conversation.
title: Ongoing Action with 在
meaning: Shows that someone is doing something right now.
when_to_use: When asking what someone is doing
When describing current activities casually
When telling someone you're busy
when_not_to_use: For completed actions (use 了)
For future actions (use 要/會)
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 在 is very common in spoken Chinese. It's less emphatic than 正在 and is the go-to structure for 'what are you doing?' questions.
word_order_notes: 在 comes after the subject and before the verb. It can be used with time words like 現在 at the beginning of the sentence.
english_contrast: English uses 'am/is/are -ing'. 在 is the direct equivalent, e.g., 'I am eating' = '我在吃'.
korean_contrast: Korean uses '~고 있다'. 在 is similar to this structure, e.g., '나는 먹고 있어' = '我在吃'.

correct_examples.translation_en: I am reading a book.

drill_examples.text: 他正在開會，等一下回你。

negative_examples.error: Negation uses 沒有, not 不. Correct: 我沒有在看書。
Negation of 在 is 沒有在. Correct: 他沒有在吃飯。

incorrect_examples.error: 在 is for present ongoing. For past ongoing, use 昨天在 or 那時候在. But 在 alone implies now.
在 is not for habits. Use 他每天跑步。

transformation_drills: Change '他看書' to ongoing: 他在看書。
Change '我吃飯' to ongoing: 我在吃飯。
production_drills: Answer: 你現在做什麼？(Use 在)
Ask a friend what they are doing using 在
common_error_patterns: Using 不 for negation of 在
Using 在 for habitual actions

repair_feedback.word_order: 在 goes before the verb: Subject + 在 + Verb.
repair_feedback.politeness: When asking someone what they are doing, start with 不好意思 or 請問 to be polite.
repair_feedback.vocabulary: Use action verbs like 吃, 看, 寫, 做 with 在.

explanation_en: Use '在忙' to say someone is busy doing something. It's a common way to explain why you can't do something right now.
title: Expressing Being Busy with 在忙
meaning: Indicates that someone is busy with an activity.
when_to_use: When you are busy and cannot talk or help
When explaining why you can't do something now
When asking if someone is free
when_not_to_use: For general statements about being busy (use 很忙)
For past busyness (use 那時候在忙)
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 在忙 is very common in daily conversation. It's often used with 不好意思 to politely decline or interrupt. For example: 不好意思，我現在在忙。
word_order_notes: 在忙 can be used alone or followed by a verb phrase. For example: 我在忙工作 or 我在忙著寫功課.
english_contrast: English says 'I'm busy' or 'I'm busy doing something'. 在忙 covers both, e.g., '我在忙' = 'I'm busy', '我在忙工作' = 'I'm busy with work'.
korean_contrast: Korean uses '바쁘다' for busy. 在忙 is like '바쁘게 ~하고 있다' (busy doing something).

correct_examples.text: 你現在在做什麼？
correct_examples.pinyin: Nǐ xiànzài zài zuò shénme?
correct_examples.translation_en: What are you doing now?

correct_examples.text: 我正在上網。
correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒ zhèngzài shàngwǎng.
correct_examples.translation_en: I am browsing the internet.

correct_examples.text: 他在忙著寫功課。
correct_examples.pinyin: Tā zài mángzhe xiě gōngkè.
correct_examples.translation_en: He is busy doing homework.

correct_examples.text: 她正在滑手機。
correct_examples.pinyin: Tā zhèngzài huá shǒujī.
correct_examples.translation_en: She is scrolling on her phone.

correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒ xiànzài zài máng, děng yīxià zài huí nǐ xùnxí.
correct_examples.translation_en: I am busy now, I'll reply to your message later.

correct_examples.text: 你現在有空嗎？

correct_examples.translation_en: Sorry, I am busy right now.

negative_examples.error: This is correct for 'I'm not busy'. But if you mean 'I'm not busy doing something', use 我沒有在忙什麼。
This is correct for past. But if you mean 'he was busy yesterday', it's fine. No error here.

incorrect_examples.error: Word order is wrong. Use 我在忙吃飯 or 我在忙著吃飯。
Negation of 在忙 is 沒有在忙. Correct: 他沒有在忙。

transformation_drills: Change '我忙' to ongoing: 我在忙。
Change '他工作' to busy with work: 他在忙工作。

Explain why you can't talk now using 在忙
common_error_patterns: Using 不 instead of 沒有 for negation
Putting 忙 after the verb instead of before

repair_feedback.word_order: 在忙 comes before the verb phrase: Subject + 在忙 + (Verb + Object).
repair_feedback.politeness: When saying you're busy, add 不好意思 to be polite: 不好意思，我現在在忙。
repair_feedback.vocabulary: Use 在忙 with activities like 工作, 寫功課, 開會.

explanation_en: Use 會 to express ability or skill, meaning 'can' or 'know how to'. It indicates that you have learned or acquired the ability to do something.
title: Expressing Ability with 會
meaning: Indicates that someone knows how to do something or has the skill to do it.
when_to_use: When talking about skills you have learned, like languages, sports, or cooking
When asking if someone can do something
When describing your abilities in a positive way
when_not_to_use: Do not use 會 for temporary possibility (use 可以 instead)
Do not use 會 for future plans (use 要 or 打算 instead)
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 會 is commonly used in daily conversation to talk about skills. It's polite to ask '你會說中文嗎？' when meeting new people.
word_order_notes: 會 comes directly after the subject and before the verb. The object follows the verb.
english_contrast: In English, 'can' is used for both ability and permission. In Chinese, 會 is only for learned ability, not permission.
korean_contrast: In Korean, '-(으)ㄹ 수 있다' is used for ability, similar to 會. However, 會 specifically implies learned skill, while Korean can also use '할 줄 알다' for learned ability.

correct_examples.text: 我會說一點中文。
correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒ huì shuō yīdiǎn zhōngwén.
correct_examples.translation_en: I can speak a little Chinese.

correct_examples.text: 他不太會寫字，可是很努力練習。
correct_examples.pinyin: Tā bù tài huì xiě zì, kěshì hěn nǔlì liànxí.
correct_examples.translation_en: He isn't very good at writing characters, but he practices very hard.

correct_examples.text: 我完全不會開車，所以打算去學。
correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒ wánquán bù huì kāichē, suǒyǐ dǎsuàn qù xué.
correct_examples.translation_en: I can't drive at all, so I plan to learn.

correct_examples.translation_en: She already knows how to swim, and she's swimming faster and faster.

correct_examples.text: 我們還不太會說中文，可是慢慢學就會進步。
correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒmen hái bù tài huì shuō zhōngwén, kěshì mànman xué jiù huì jìnbù.

correct_examples.text: 他會唱歌，也會跳舞，可是不會彈鋼琴。

Ask a friend: '你 會 煮菜 嗎？'
common_error_patterns: Using 會 for permission instead of 可以
Using 會 for future plans instead of 要

repair_feedback.word_order: Make sure 會 is placed before the verb, not after.
repair_feedback.politeness: Using 會 in questions is polite and natural.
repair_feedback.vocabulary: Use 會 only for learned abilities, not for temporary situations.

pattern: Subject + 不 會 + Verb + Object
explanation_en: Use 不 會 to express that you do not know how to do something or lack the ability. It is the negative form of 會.
title: Negating Ability with 不 會
meaning: Indicates that someone does not know how to do something or lacks the skill.
when_to_use: When you want to say you cannot do something because you haven't learned it
When answering negatively to a question about ability
When describing what you are not capable of
when_not_to_use: Do not use 不 會 for things that are impossible due to external reasons (use 不能 instead)
Do not use 不 會 for things you don't want to do (use 不想 instead)
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, it's common to say '我 不 會 說 中文' when you are a beginner. It's polite to add '還 在 學' to show you are learning.
word_order_notes: 不 會 comes directly after the subject and before the verb. The object follows the verb.
english_contrast: In English, 'I can't' can mean both lack of ability and impossibility. In Chinese, 不 會 is specifically for lack of learned ability.
korean_contrast: In Korean, '못 하다' or '할 줄 모르다' is used for lack of ability, similar to 不 會. However, 不 會 is more specific to learned skills.

pattern: Subject + 還 不 太 會 + Verb + Object
structure: 還 不 太 會
explanation_en: Use 還 不 太 會 to express that you are still learning and not yet proficient. It indicates partial ability or ongoing learning.
title: Expressing Partial Ability with 還 不 太 會
meaning: Indicates that someone is still learning and cannot do something well yet.
when_to_use: When you are a beginner and still learning a skill
When you want to be humble about your ability
When describing progress in learning
when_not_to_use: Do not use 還 不 太 會 if you are already proficient (use 會 instead)
Do not use 還 不 太 會 for things you have never tried (use 不 會 instead)
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, saying '我 還 不 太 會 說 中文' is a humble and polite way to indicate you are learning. It encourages others to be patient and helpful.
word_order_notes: 還 不 太 會 is a fixed phrase that comes after the subject and before the verb. The object follows the verb.
english_contrast: In English, 'I'm not very good at it yet' conveys a similar meaning. 還 不 太 會 is more specific to ongoing learning.
korean_contrast: In Korean, '아직 잘 못 해요' is similar, meaning 'not yet good at it'. Both imply ongoing learning and humility.

drill_examples.translation_ko: 제 친구는 아직 수영을 잘 못해서 다음 주에 배우러 갈 계획이에요.

drill_examples.text: 她還不太會說英文，可是她已經聽懂很多了。
drill_examples.pinyin: Tā hái bù tài huì shuō Yīngwén, kěshì tā yǐjīng tīng dǒng hěn duō le.
drill_examples.translation_en: She still can't speak English very well, but she already understands a lot.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 그녀는 아직 영어를 잘 말하지 못하지만, 이미 많이 알아들어요.

drill_examples.text: 我哥哥還不太會開車，他覺得開車很難。
drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ gēge hái bù tài huì kāi chē, tā juéde kāi chē hěn nán.
drill_examples.translation_en: My older brother still can't drive very well; he thinks driving is difficult.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 제 오빠는 아직 운전을 잘 못해요. 운전이 어렵다고 생각해요.

drill_examples.text: 你還會不太會用這個手機嗎？我可以幫你。
drill_examples.pinyin: Nǐ hái bù tài huì yòng zhège shǒujī ma? Wǒ kěyǐ bāng nǐ.
drill_examples.translation_en: Do you still not know how to use this phone very well? I can help you.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 아직 이 핸드폰 사용을 잘 못하세요? 제가 도와드릴 수 있어요.

negative_examples.text: 我 還 不 太 會 說 中文。
negative_examples.error: This is correct. No negative example needed here.

incorrect_examples.text: 我 還 不 太 會 游泳 昨天。
incorrect_examples.error: 還 不 太 會 is for present ongoing learning, not past. Use 不 會 for past.

transformation_drills: Change '我 不 會 說 中文' to show ongoing learning: '我 還 不 太 會 說 中文'
Change '他 會 游泳' to show partial ability: '他 還 不 太 會 游泳'
production_drills: Tell a friend: '我 還 不 太 會 煮菜，但是 我 在 學'
Answer: '你 會 說 中文 嗎？' (Partial: 還 不 太 會，但是 我 慢慢 學)
common_error_patterns: Using 還 不 太 會 for things you have no ability at all
Using 還 不 太 會 for past situations

repair_feedback.word_order: 還 不 太 會 is a fixed phrase; do not separate the words.
repair_feedback.politeness: Using 還 不 太 會 shows humility and is appreciated in Taiwan.
repair_feedback.vocabulary: Use 還 不 太 會 only when you have some ability but are still learning.

pattern: Subject + 長得 / 看起來 + Adjective
explanation_en: Use 長得 to describe inherent physical traits (height, build, etc.) and 看起來 for perceived appearance or impression.
title: Describing Appearance with 長得 and 看起來
meaning: Describing how someone looks
when_to_use: Describing height, build, or facial features
Giving first impressions of someone's appearance
when_not_to_use: Describing personality (use 個性 instead)
Describing temporary states like clothing (use 穿著)
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 長得 is common for physical traits; 看起來 is softer and often used for compliments like '你看起來很年輕'.
word_order_notes: Adjective always comes after 長得 or 看起來, never before.
english_contrast: English says 'He is tall' directly; Chinese requires 長得 before the adjective.
korean_contrast: Korean uses '~이/가 키가 크다' structure; Chinese uses 長得 + adjective.

correct_examples.text: 他長得很高，而且很瘦。
correct_examples.pinyin: Tā zhǎng de hěn gāo, érqiě hěn shòu.
correct_examples.translation_en: He is tall and thin.

correct_examples.text: 她看起來很年輕，可是已經三十歲了。
correct_examples.pinyin: Tā kàn qǐlái hěn niánqīng, kěshì yǐjīng sānshí suì le.
correct_examples.translation_en: She looks very young, but she is already thirty years old.

correct_examples.text: 那個人的頭髮很長，而且戴眼鏡。
correct_examples.pinyin: Nàge rén de tóufa hěn cháng, érqiě dài yǎnjìng.
correct_examples.translation_en: That person has long hair and wears glasses.

correct_examples.text: 我妹妹長得很可愛，個性也很活潑。
correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒ mèimei zhǎng de hěn kě'ài, gèxìng yě hěn huópō.
correct_examples.translation_en: My younger sister looks cute and has a lively personality.

correct_examples.text: 他看起來很友善，可是說話不多。
correct_examples.pinyin: Tā kàn qǐlái hěn yǒushàn, kěshì shuōhuà bù duō.
correct_examples.translation_en: He looks friendly, but he doesn't talk much.

correct_examples.text: 我哥哥長得很帥，而且很幽默。
correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒ gēge zhǎng de hěn shuài, érqiě hěn yōumò.
correct_examples.translation_en: My older brother is handsome and very humorous.

correct_examples.text: 那個女生看起來很溫柔，可是個性很認真。
correct_examples.pinyin: Nàge nǚshēng kàn qǐlái hěn wēnróu, kěshì gèxìng hěn rènzhēn.
correct_examples.translation_en: That girl looks gentle, but she has a serious personality.

correct_examples.text: 他長得不高，可是很聰明。
correct_examples.pinyin: Tā zhǎng de bù gāo, kěshì hěn cōngmíng.
correct_examples.translation_en: He is not tall, but he is very smart.

drill_examples.text: 他長得很高，而且很瘦。
drill_examples.pinyin: Tā zhǎng de hěn gāo, érqiě hěn shòu.
drill_examples.translation_en: He is tall and thin.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 그는 키가 크고 날씬합니다.

drill_examples.text: 她看起來很年輕，可是已經三十歲了。
drill_examples.pinyin: Tā kàn qǐlái hěn niánqīng, kěshì yǐjīng sānshí suì le.
drill_examples.translation_en: She looks very young, but she is already thirty years old.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 그녀는 아주 젊어 보이지만, 이미 서른 살입니다.

drill_examples.text: 那個人的頭髮很長，而且戴眼鏡。
drill_examples.pinyin: Nàge rén de tóufa hěn cháng, érqiě dài yǎnjìng.
drill_examples.translation_en: That person has long hair and wears glasses.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 그 사람은 머리가 길고 안경을 쓰고 있습니다.

drill_examples.text: 我妹妹長得很可愛，個性也很活潑。
drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ mèimei zhǎng de hěn kě'ài, gèxìng yě hěn huópō.
drill_examples.translation_en: My younger sister looks cute and has a lively personality.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 제 여동생은 귀엽게 생겼고 성격도 활발합니다.

drill_examples.text: 他看起來很友善，可是說話不多。
drill_examples.pinyin: Tā kàn qǐlái hěn yǒushàn, kěshì shuōhuà bù duō.
drill_examples.translation_en: He looks friendly, but he doesn't talk much.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 그는 친근해 보이지만 말을 많이 하지 않습니다.

drill_examples.text: 我哥哥長得很帥，而且很幽默。
drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ gēge zhǎng de hěn shuài, érqiě hěn yōumò.
drill_examples.translation_en: My older brother is handsome and very humorous.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 제 오빠는 잘생겼고 아주 유머러스합니다.

drill_examples.text: 那個女生看起來很溫柔，可是個性很認真。
drill_examples.pinyin: Nàge nǚshēng kàn qǐlái hěn wēnróu, kěshì gèxìng hěn rènzhēn.
drill_examples.translation_en: That girl looks gentle, but she has a serious personality.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 그 여학생은 부드러워 보이지만 성격은 아주 진지합니다.

drill_examples.text: 他長得不高，可是很聰明。
drill_examples.pinyin: Tā zhǎng de bù gāo, kěshì hěn cōngmíng.
drill_examples.translation_en: He is not tall, but he is very smart.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 그는 키가 크지 않지만 아주 똑똑합니다.

negative_examples.error: Adjective must come after 長得, not before.

incorrect_examples.error: Correct! But if you meant '他很高', that's a different structure. 長得高 is fine for height.

transformation_drills: Change '他很高' to '他長得很高'
Change '她看起來很年輕' to '她長得很年輕'
production_drills: Describe your friend's height using 長得
Describe how someone seems today using 看起來
common_error_patterns: Omitting 得 after 長
Using 長得 for personality

repair_feedback.word_order: Remember: 長得/看起來 + adjective, not adjective + 長得/看起來.
repair_feedback.politeness: Using 看起來 is more polite for compliments than direct 長得.
repair_feedback.vocabulary: Use 長得 for physical traits, 看起來 for impressions.

explanation_en: Use 很 before adjectives to describe personality traits. 很 is neutral here, not meaning 'very'.
title: Describing Personality with 很 + Adjective
meaning: Describing someone's personality
when_to_use: Describing character traits like 友善, 幽默, 認真
Making general statements about someone's nature
when_not_to_use: Describing physical appearance (use 長得)
Emphasizing 'very' (use 非常 or 很 with stress)
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 很 is often used as a linking verb, not intensifier. Saying '他友善' sounds incomplete; '他很友善' is natural.
word_order_notes: Adjective always follows 很, never before the subject.
english_contrast: English says 'He is friendly'; Chinese requires 很 before the adjective: '他很友善'.
korean_contrast: Korean uses '그는 친절해요' without a separate word; Chinese needs 很.

correct_examples.text: 那個人長得很高，也很瘦。
correct_examples.pinyin: Nà ge rén zhǎng de hěn gāo, yě hěn shòu.
correct_examples.translation_en: That person is very tall and also very thin.

correct_examples.text: 我妹妹的個性很活潑，她喜歡說話。
correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒ mèimei de gèxìng hěn huópō, tā xǐhuān shuōhuà.
correct_examples.translation_en: My younger sister's personality is very lively; she likes to talk.

correct_examples.text: 他戴眼鏡，看起來很聰明。
correct_examples.pinyin: Tā dài yǎnjìng, kàn qǐlái hěn cōngmíng.
correct_examples.translation_en: He wears glasses and looks very smart.

correct_examples.text: 我認識一個很幽默的朋友，他常常讓我們笑。
correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒ rènshi yī ge hěn yōumò de péngyou, tā chángcháng ràng wǒmen xiào.
correct_examples.translation_en: I know a very humorous friend; he often makes us laugh.

correct_examples.text: 那個女生穿著白色的衣服，長得很漂亮。
correct_examples.pinyin: Nà ge nǚshēng chuān zhe báisè de yīfu, zhǎng de hěn piàoliang.
correct_examples.translation_en: That girl is wearing white clothes and looks very pretty.

correct_examples.text: 我哥哥的個性很認真，他工作很努力。
correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒ gēge de gèxìng hěn rènzhēn, tā gōngzuò hěn nǔlì.
correct_examples.translation_en: My older brother's personality is very serious; he works very hard.

correct_examples.text: 聽說那個新同學很害羞，不太說話。
correct_examples.pinyin: Tīngshuō nà ge xīn tóngxué hěn hàixiū, bù tài shuōhuà.
correct_examples.translation_en: I heard that new classmate is very shy and doesn't talk much.

correct_examples.text: 他長得不高，可是他的個性很大方。
correct_examples.pinyin: Tā zhǎng de bù gāo, kěshì tā de gèxìng hěn dàfāng.
correct_examples.translation_en: He is not tall, but his personality is very generous.

drill_examples.text: 那個人長得很高，也很瘦。

drill_examples.text: 我妹妹的個性很活潑，她喜歡說話。

drill_examples.translation_ko: 제 여동생의 성격은 매우 활발해서 말하기를 좋아합니다.

drill_examples.text: 他戴眼鏡，看起來很聰明。
drill_examples.pinyin: Tā dài yǎnjìng, kàn qǐlái hěn cōngmíng.
drill_examples.translation_en: He wears glasses and looks very smart.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 그는 안경을 쓰고 있어서 매우 똑똑해 보입니다.

drill_examples.text: 我認識一個很幽默的朋友，他常常讓我們笑。
drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ rènshi yī ge hěn yōumò de péngyou, tā chángcháng ràng wǒmen xiào.
drill_examples.translation_en: I know a very humorous friend; he often makes us laugh.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 저는 매우 유머러스한 친구를 알고 있는데, 그는 자주 우리를 웃게 합니다.

drill_examples.text: 那個女生穿著白色的衣服，長得很漂亮。
drill_examples.pinyin: Nà ge nǚshēng chuān zhe báisè de yīfu, zhǎng de hěn piàoliang.
drill_examples.translation_en: That girl is wearing white clothes and looks very pretty.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 그 여학생은 흰색 옷을 입고 있으며, 매우 예쁘게 생겼습니다.

drill_examples.text: 我哥哥的個性很認真，他工作很努力。
drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ gēge de gèxìng hěn rènzhēn, tā gōngzuò hěn nǔlì.
drill_examples.translation_en: My older brother's personality is very serious; he works very hard.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 제 오빠의 성격은 매우 진지해서 일을 열심히 합니다.

drill_examples.text: 聽說那個新同學很害羞，不太說話。
drill_examples.pinyin: Tīngshuō nà ge xīn tóngxué hěn hàixiū, bù tài shuōhuà.
drill_examples.translation_en: I heard that new classmate is very shy and doesn't talk much.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 그 새 친구는 매우 수줍어해서 말을 많이 하지 않는다고 들었습니다.

drill_examples.text: 他長得不高，可是他的個性很大方。
drill_examples.pinyin: Tā zhǎng de bù gāo, kěshì tā de gèxìng hěn dàfāng.
drill_examples.translation_en: He is not tall, but his personality is very generous.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 그는 키가 크지 않지만, 성격은 매우 대범합니다.

negative_examples.error: Missing 很 makes the sentence sound unnatural or incomplete.

incorrect_examples.error: This is correct! But if you meant 'very friendly', stress 很 in speech.

transformation_drills: Add 很 to '他聰明' to make it natural
Change '她很認真' to a question form
production_drills: Describe your teacher's personality using 很 + adjective
Say your friend is funny using 幽默
common_error_patterns: Omitting 很 before adjectives
Using 很 for physical traits instead of 長得

repair_feedback.word_order: Always put 很 before the adjective, not after.
repair_feedback.politeness: Using 很 is standard; omitting it can sound blunt.
repair_feedback.vocabulary: Use personality adjectives like 友善, 幽默, 認真 with 很.

pattern: 那個人 + 是 + 誰 / 我朋友 / 他
explanation_en: Use 那個人 to refer to a specific person, then identify them with 是.
title: Identifying People with 那個人
meaning: Identifying a specific person
when_to_use: Pointing out someone in a group
Asking or answering who someone is
when_not_to_use: Describing appearance (use 長得)
Talking about yourself (use 我)
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 那個人 is common in casual conversation. For politeness, add 請問 before questions.
word_order_notes: 那個人 comes first, then 是, then the identifier.
english_contrast: English says 'That person is my friend'; Chinese order is same: 那個人是我朋友.
korean_contrast: Korean uses '그 사람은 내 친구예요'; Chinese uses 那個人 + 是.

correct_examples.text: 那個人是誰？
correct_examples.pinyin: Nà ge rén shì shuí?
correct_examples.translation_en: Who is that person?

correct_examples.text: 那個人是我朋友，他長得很高。
correct_examples.pinyin: Nà ge rén shì wǒ péngyǒu, tā zhǎng de hěn gāo.
correct_examples.translation_en: That person is my friend; he is very tall.

correct_examples.text: 那個人看起來很年輕，可是已經三十歲了。

correct_examples.translation_en: That person looks very young, but is already thirty years old.

correct_examples.translation_en: That person is wearing black clothes and glasses; he is very handsome.

correct_examples.pinyin: Nà ge rén shì wǒ mèimei, tā gèxìng hěn huópō, xǐhuān shuōhuà.
correct_examples.translation_en: That person is my younger sister; she has a very lively personality and likes to talk.

correct_examples.text: 那個人長得很可愛，而且很友善。
correct_examples.pinyin: Nà ge rén zhǎng de hěn kě'ài, érqiě hěn yǒushàn.
correct_examples.translation_en: That person looks very cute and is also very friendly.

correct_examples.text: 那個人是老師嗎？他看起來很認真。

negative_examples.error: 那個人 must come before 是, not after.

incorrect_examples.error: This is correct! But if you meant 'That tall person', use 那個人長得很高.

transformation_drills: Change '那個人是我朋友' to a question
production_drills: Point to someone and ask who they are
Identify a person in a photo using 那個人
common_error_patterns: Using 這 instead of 那 for distant people
Omitting 是

repair_feedback.word_order: 那個人 + 是 + identifier, not identifier + 是 + 那個人.
repair_feedback.politeness: Add 請問 for polite questions: '請問那個人是誰？'
repair_feedback.vocabulary: Use 那個人 for specific people, not for objects.

explanation_en: Use '比' to compare two items, stating that A is more [adjective] than B.
title: Basic Comparison with 比
meaning: A is more [adjective] than B
when_to_use: Comparing prices, sizes, distances, or qualities of two items
Stating a clear difference between two options
when_not_to_use: When the difference is small or uncertain
When comparing more than two items at once
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, '比' is very common in daily conversation, especially when discussing food or shopping choices. It's neutral and polite.
word_order_notes: The structure is fixed: A + 比 + B + adjective. Do not add '很' or other degree adverbs before the adjective.
english_contrast: In English, we say 'A is more [adjective] than B'. In Chinese, the word order is A + 比 + B + adjective, without 'more'.
korean_contrast: In Korean, you use 'A가 B보다 더 [adjective]' (A-ga B-boda deo [adjective]). The Chinese structure is similar but without '더' (deo).

correct_examples.text: 這家餐廳的牛肉麵比那家好吃。
correct_examples.pinyin: Zhè jiā cāntīng de niúròumiàn bǐ nà jiā hǎochī.
correct_examples.translation_en: The beef noodles at this restaurant are tastier than those at that one.

correct_examples.text: 便利商店的咖啡比咖啡店便宜很多。
correct_examples.pinyin: Biànlì shāngdiàn de kāfēi bǐ kāfēidiàn piányí hěnduō.
correct_examples.translation_en: The coffee at convenience stores is much cheaper than at coffee shops.

correct_examples.text: 坐捷運比坐公車快，也比較方便。
correct_examples.pinyin: Zuò jiéyùn bǐ zuò gōngchē kuài, yě bǐjiào fāngbiàn.
correct_examples.translation_en: Taking the MRT is faster than taking the bus, and also more convenient.

correct_examples.text: 夜市的滷肉飯比小吃店的便宜，可是份量比較少。
correct_examples.pinyin: Yèshì de lǔròufàn bǐ xiǎochīdiàn de piányí, kěshì fènliàng bǐjiào shǎo.
correct_examples.translation_en: The braised pork rice at the night market is cheaper than at the snack shop, but the portion is smaller.

correct_examples.text: 這家店的服務態度比那家好很多。
correct_examples.pinyin: Zhè jiā diàn de fúwù tàidù bǐ nà jiā hǎo hěnduō.
correct_examples.translation_en: The service attitude at this shop is much better than at that one.

correct_examples.text: 同樣的價格，我覺得買這個比較划算。
correct_examples.pinyin: Tóngyàng de jiàgé, wǒ juéde mǎi zhège bǐjiào huásuàn.
correct_examples.translation_en: At the same price, I think buying this one is more cost-effective.

correct_examples.text: 這裡的環境比那裡安靜，比較適合看書。
correct_examples.pinyin: Zhèlǐ de huánjìng bǐ nàlǐ ānjìng, bǐjiào shìhé kànshū.
correct_examples.translation_en: The environment here is quieter than there, and more suitable for reading.

correct_examples.text: 珍珠奶茶的甜度，半糖比正常甜好喝。
correct_examples.pinyin: Zhēnzhū nǎichá de tiándù, bàntáng bǐ zhèngcháng tián hǎohē.
correct_examples.translation_en: For the sweetness of bubble milk tea, half sugar tastes better than regular sweetness.

values.meaning_en: almost the same; about the same; not much difference

pattern: A 跟 B 差不多
explanation_en: Use '跟...差不多' to say that two items are similar or almost the same in a certain aspect.
title: Similarity with 差不多
meaning: A and B are about the same
when_to_use: When two items have similar prices, sizes, or qualities
When you want to say there's no big difference
when_not_to_use: When there is a clear difference
When you want to emphasize one is better
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, '差不多' is very common in casual conversation, especially when comparing food or prices. It's a soft way to say 'no big difference'.
word_order_notes: The structure is: A + 跟 + B + 差不多. You can add a noun after '差不多' if needed, e.g., 'A 跟 B 差不多貴'.
english_contrast: In English, we say 'A and B are about the same'. In Chinese, the structure is A + 跟 + B + 差不多.
korean_contrast: In Korean, you use 'A와 B가 비슷하다' (A-wa B-ga biseuthada). The Chinese structure is similar but uses '差不多' instead of '비슷하다'.

correct_examples.translation_en: The price at this restaurant is about the same as that one.

values.meaning_en: almost the same; about the same; not much difference

explanation_en: Use '沒有' to express that A is not as [adjective] as B.
title: Negative Comparison with 沒有
meaning: A is not as [adjective] as B
when_to_use: When you want to say A is less [adjective] than B
When comparing qualities like price, size, or distance
when_not_to_use: When A is more [adjective] than B (use 比 instead)
When the comparison is about equality (use 差不多 instead)
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, '沒有' is commonly used in negative comparisons, especially when discussing prices or convenience. It's polite and clear.
word_order_notes: The structure is: A + 沒有 + B + adjective. Do not add '很' before the adjective.
english_contrast: In English, we say 'A is not as [adjective] as B'. In Chinese, the structure is A + 沒有 + B + adjective.
korean_contrast: In Korean, you use 'A가 B만큼 [adjective]하지 않다' (A-ga B-mankeum [adjective]haji anta). The Chinese structure is simpler with '沒有'.

repair_feedback.word_order: Remember: A + 沒有 + B + adjective, no '很'.
repair_feedback.politeness: This structure is neutral and polite.
repair_feedback.vocabulary: Use adjectives like 貴, 便宜, 近, 遠, 方便, 麻煩.

values.meaning_en: almost the same; about the same; not much difference

explanation_en: Use '要不要' to make a friendly invitation or suggestion, meaning 'do you want to...' or 'how about...'. It's a common and polite way to propose an activity in Taiwan.
title: Making Invitations with 要不要
meaning: Do you want to...? / How about...?
when_to_use: Inviting someone to do an activity together
Suggesting a plan in a casual or friendly context
Checking if someone is interested in an idea
when_not_to_use: Do not use for formal or very polite invitations (use 請 or 不好意思 instead)
Do not use when the action is already decided or mandatory
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 要不要 is very common among friends and acquaintances. It sounds natural and friendly, not pushy. You can add 一起 (together) after the verb to emphasize joint activity.
word_order_notes: The structure is fixed: 要不要 + verb + object. The subject is often omitted if clear from context. For example: 要不要喝咖啡？(Do you want to drink coffee?)
english_contrast: English uses 'Do you want to...' or 'Would you like to...' with a similar structure, but 要不要 is more direct and casual, similar to 'Wanna...'.
korean_contrast: Korean uses '~을래요?' or '~할까요?' for invitations. 要不要 is similar to '~을래요?' in casual tone.

correct_examples.text: 你要不要跟我一起去咖啡店？
correct_examples.pinyin: Nǐ yào bù yào gēn wǒ yīqǐ qù kāfēidiàn?
correct_examples.translation_en: Would you like to go to the coffee shop with me?

correct_examples.text: 好啊，我們約幾點？
correct_examples.pinyin: Hǎo a, wǒmen yuē jǐ diǎn?
correct_examples.translation_en: Sure, what time should we meet?

correct_examples.text: 要不要改天再約？我今天臨時有事。
correct_examples.pinyin: Yào bù yào gǎitiān zài yuē? Wǒ jīntiān línshí yǒu shì.
correct_examples.translation_en: How about we reschedule for another day? I have something unexpected today.

correct_examples.text: 你明天有空嗎？要不要一起去看電影？
correct_examples.pinyin: Nǐ míngtiān yǒu kòng ma? Yào bù yào yīqǐ qù kàn diànyǐng?
correct_examples.translation_en: Are you free tomorrow? Would you like to go watch a movie together?

correct_examples.text: 不好意思，我這幾天很忙，沒辦法去。
correct_examples.pinyin: Bù hǎoyìsi, wǒ zhè jǐ tiān hěn máng, méi bànfǎ qù.
correct_examples.translation_en: Sorry, I've been very busy these days and can't make it.

correct_examples.text: 我們要不要提早到餐廳門口碰面？
correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒmen yào bù yào tízǎo dào cāntīng ménkǒu pèngmiàn?
correct_examples.translation_en: Should we meet at the restaurant entrance earlier?

correct_examples.text: 沒問題啊，我們約好時間再聯絡。
correct_examples.pinyin: Méi wèntí a, wǒmen yuē hǎo shíjiān zài liánluò.
correct_examples.translation_en: No problem, let's set a time and then get in touch.

correct_examples.text: 你要不要取消今天的約會？因為臨時下雨了。
correct_examples.pinyin: Nǐ yào bù yào qǔxiāo jīntiān de yuēhuì? Yīnwèi línshí xià yǔ le.
correct_examples.translation_en: Would you like to cancel today's date? Because it suddenly started raining.

negative_examples.error: Incorrect word order. The subject should not be placed after 要不要. Correct: 你要不要喝咖啡？
Incorrect word order. The verb must come directly after 要不要, and the object after the verb. Correct: 你要不要喝咖啡？

incorrect_examples.error: Incorrect word order. The verb should be before the object. Correct: 要不要喝咖啡？
Incomplete. You need to specify the activity. Correct: 要不要一起去？ or 要不要一起喝咖啡？

transformation_drills: Change '你要不要喝咖啡？' to a question about eating noodles: '你要不要吃麵？'
Change '要不要看電影？' to include '一起': '要不要一起看電影？'
production_drills: Invite a friend to go to a coffee shop using 要不要.
Ask if someone wants to watch a movie this weekend.
common_error_patterns: Placing the subject after 要不要 instead of before it
Omitting the verb and using only object after 要不要

repair_feedback.word_order: Remember: 要不要 comes first, then the verb, then the object. Subject can be before 要不要 or omitted.
repair_feedback.politeness: This structure is casual. For more polite invitations, use 請 or 不好意思.
repair_feedback.vocabulary: Use appropriate verbs like 喝, 吃, 看, 去, 來.

pattern: Subject + 把 + time/date + 改到 + new time/date
explanation_en: Use '把...改到...' to express changing or rescheduling a time or date. This structure emphasizes the object (time) being changed to a new one.
title: Rescheduling with 把...改到...
meaning: Change/reschedule (time) to (new time)
when_to_use: When you need to change the time or date of a plan
When proposing a new time for a meeting or appointment
When informing someone about a schedule change
when_not_to_use: Do not use for changing objects or locations (use 換 or 改 for those)
Do not use if you are canceling the plan entirely (use 取消 instead)
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, it's common to use 改時間 or 改期 for rescheduling. Using 把 adds emphasis on the change. You can also say '我們把時間改到...' to include the group.
word_order_notes: The structure is: Subject + 把 + original time/date + 改到 + new time/date. The original time must be specified, and the new time follows 改到.
english_contrast: English uses 'change/reschedule to' with a direct object. Chinese uses 把 to mark the object being changed, which is different from English word order.
korean_contrast: Korean uses '~을/를 ~로 바꾸다' similar to 把...改到... structure, but Korean places the object marker before the verb.

correct_examples.text: 我們把約會改到明天下午好嗎？
correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒmen bǎ yuēhuì gǎi dào míngtiān xiàwǔ hǎo ma?
correct_examples.translation_en: Shall we reschedule the date to tomorrow afternoon?

correct_examples.text: 因為臨時有事，我把時間改到星期五了。
correct_examples.pinyin: Yīnwèi línshí yǒu shì, wǒ bǎ shíjiān gǎi dào xīngqīwǔ le.
correct_examples.translation_en: Because something came up, I rescheduled the time to Friday.

correct_examples.text: 他把行程從今天改到這個週末了。
correct_examples.pinyin: Tā bǎ xíngchéng cóng jīntiān gǎi dào zhège zhōumò le.
correct_examples.translation_en: He rescheduled the itinerary from today to this weekend.

correct_examples.text: 我想把見面的日期改到下個星期一。
correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒ xiǎng bǎ jiànmiàn de rìqī gǎi dào xiàge xīngqīyī.
correct_examples.translation_en: I want to reschedule the meeting date to next Monday.

correct_examples.text: 抱歉，我臨時沒空，可以把時間改到晚上嗎？
correct_examples.pinyin: Bàoqiàn, wǒ línshí méi kòng, kěyǐ bǎ shíjiān gǎi dào wǎnshàng ma?
correct_examples.translation_en: Sorry, I'm suddenly unavailable; can we reschedule the time to the evening?

correct_examples.text: 我們把咖啡店的約會改到明天早上吧。
correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒmen bǎ kāfēidiàn de yuēhuì gǎi dào míngtiān zǎoshang ba.
correct_examples.translation_en: Let's reschedule the coffee shop date to tomorrow morning.

correct_examples.text: 你方便把時間提早到下午兩點嗎？
correct_examples.pinyin: Nǐ fāngbiàn bǎ shíjiān tízǎo dào xiàwǔ liǎng diǎn ma?
correct_examples.translation_en: Is it convenient for you to move the time earlier to 2 PM?

correct_examples.text: 因為颱風，我們把電影院的約會取消了。
correct_examples.pinyin: Yīnwèi táifēng, wǒmen bǎ diànyǐngyuàn de yuēhuì qǔxiāo le.
correct_examples.translation_en: Because of the typhoon, we canceled the movie theater date.

drill_examples.text: 抱歉，我臨時沒空，可以把時間改到晚上嗎？

drill_examples.text: 因為颱風，我們把電影院的約會取消了。

negative_examples.error: Incorrect word order. The original time must come before 改到. Correct: 我把時間改到明天。
Missing 把 and incorrect word order. Correct: 我把時間改到明天。

incorrect_examples.error: Missing 到 after 改. Correct: 我把時間改到明天。
This is acceptable but less common. The 把 structure is more natural for emphasis. Correct: 我把時間改到明天。

transformation_drills: Change '我把時間改到明天' to change the date to next week: '我把時間改到下個星期。'

Reschedule a coffee date from today to tomorrow.
common_error_patterns: Forgetting 到 after 改
Placing the new time before 改到
Omitting the original time object

repair_feedback.word_order: Remember: 把 + original time + 改到 + new time. The original time is the object of 把.
repair_feedback.politeness: When rescheduling, it's polite to apologize first, e.g., '不好意思，我把時間改到...'
repair_feedback.vocabulary: Use time words like 明天, 下個星期, 星期五, 三點.

pattern: 不好意思，subject + 不能 + verb + (object) + 了
explanation_en: Use '不好意思...不能...了' to politely decline an invitation or cancel a plan. The 了 indicates a change of situation, and 不好意思 softens the refusal.
title: Politely Declining with 不好意思...不能...了
meaning: Sorry, (subject) can't (verb) anymore
when_to_use: When you need to decline an invitation politely
When canceling a previously agreed plan
When explaining that you are unable to do something due to a change
when_not_to_use: Do not use for simple refusals without a reason (use 不用了 or 沒關係 instead)
Do not use if you are just saying no to a suggestion (use 不要 or 不想 instead)
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 不好意思 is a key politeness marker. Adding a reason after 了 makes the refusal more acceptable, e.g., '不好意思，我不能去了，因為臨時有事。'
word_order_notes: Start with 不好意思, then subject, then 不能 + verb + object, and end with 了. The reason can follow after 了.
english_contrast: English uses 'Sorry, I can't...' with similar structure, but Chinese requires 了 to show the change, which English does not have.
korean_contrast: Korean uses '죄송하지만 ~을/를 못 하게 됐어요' which includes a similar apology and change marker.

drill_examples.translation_en: I'm sorry, we can't cancel this appointment.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 죄송합니다만, 이 약속을 취소할 수 없어요.

negative_examples.error: This is correct but missing 了. Adding 了 makes it clear the situation has changed. Correct: 不好意思，我不能去了。
Incorrect word order. 了 must come after the verb phrase. Correct: 不好意思，我不能去了。

incorrect_examples.error: Incorrect word order. 不能 must stay together. Correct: 不好意思，我不能去了。

incorrect_examples.text: 不好意思，我不能去咖啡店了。
incorrect_examples.error: This is correct. Ensure the object is placed after the verb.

transformation_drills: Change '不好意思，我不能去了' to decline a coffee invitation: '不好意思，我不能喝咖啡了。'
Change '不好意思，我不能看電影了' to include a reason: '不好意思，我不能看電影了，因為太忙。'
production_drills: Politely decline a dinner invitation because you are busy.
Cancel a plan to go to the park because of rain.
common_error_patterns: Omitting 了 at the end
Placing 了 before the verb
Not using 不好意思 for politeness

repair_feedback.word_order: Remember: 不好意思 + subject + 不能 + verb + object + 了. The 了 always comes at the end.
repair_feedback.politeness: Always start with 不好意思 to soften the refusal. Adding a reason makes it more polite.
repair_feedback.vocabulary: Use verbs like 去, 來, 喝, 吃, 看. Add reasons like 太忙, 臨時有事, 不舒服.

explanation_en: Use 有 to state that a room or apartment has a certain facility or feature.
title: 有 for Facility Existence
meaning: A room/apartment has [facility].
when_to_use: Describing what facilities are in a room or apartment
Listing features of a place
when_not_to_use: When describing location of an object (use 在 instead)
When talking about possession of personal items
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, when describing an apartment, it's common to list facilities like 冷氣, 洗衣機, 網路. Use 有 to confirm availability.
word_order_notes: Place the room/location first, then 有, then the facility. No measure word needed for general existence.
english_contrast: English says 'The room has air conditioning.' Chinese uses the same structure: 房間有冷氣.
korean_contrast: Korean uses '에는/에는 + 이/가 있다' structure. Chinese is simpler: just 有.

correct_examples.text: 我的公寓有一個客廳和兩個房間。
correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒ de gōngyù yǒu yī ge kètīng hé liǎng ge fángjiān.
correct_examples.translation_en: My apartment has one living room and two bedrooms.

correct_examples.text: 房間裡有一張床、一個衣櫃和一張書桌。
correct_examples.pinyin: Fángjiān lǐ yǒu yī zhāng chuáng, yī ge yīguì hé yī zhāng shūzhuō.
correct_examples.translation_en: There is a bed, a wardrobe, and a desk in the room.

correct_examples.text: 廚房裡有冰箱、洗衣機和熱水器。
correct_examples.pinyin: Chúfáng lǐ yǒu bīngxiāng, xǐyījī hé rèshuǐqì.
correct_examples.translation_en: The kitchen has a refrigerator, a washing machine, and a water heater.

correct_examples.text: 客廳有沙發、桌子和電視。
correct_examples.pinyin: Kètīng yǒu shāfā, zhuōzi hé diànshì.
correct_examples.translation_en: The living room has a sofa, a table, and a TV.

correct_examples.text: 浴室有馬桶、水槽和一個大窗戶。
correct_examples.pinyin: Yùshì yǒu mǎtǒng, shuǐcáo hé yī ge dà chuānghù.
correct_examples.translation_en: The bathroom has a toilet, a sink, and a big window.

correct_examples.text: 陽台有兩張椅子和一盞燈。
correct_examples.pinyin: Yángtái yǒu liǎng zhāng yǐzi hé yī zhǎn dēng.
correct_examples.translation_en: The balcony has two chairs and a lamp.

correct_examples.text: 這個房間沒有冷氣，但是有電風扇。
correct_examples.pinyin: Zhè ge fángjiān méiyǒu lěngqì, dànshì yǒu diànfēngshàn.
correct_examples.translation_en: This room doesn't have air conditioning, but it has an electric fan.

correct_examples.text: 公寓的門前面有一個小陽台。
correct_examples.pinyin: Gōngyù de mén qiánmiàn yǒu yī ge xiǎo yángtái.
correct_examples.translation_en: There is a small balcony in front of the apartment door.

drill_examples.text: 我的公寓有一個客廳和兩個房間。
drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ de gōngyù yǒu yī ge kètīng hé liǎng ge fángjiān.
drill_examples.translation_en: My apartment has one living room and two bedrooms.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 제 아파트에는 거실 하나와 방 두 개가 있습니다.

drill_examples.text: 房間裡有一張床、一個衣櫃和一張書桌。
drill_examples.pinyin: Fángjiān lǐ yǒu yī zhāng chuáng, yī ge yīguì hé yī zhāng shūzhuō.
drill_examples.translation_en: There is a bed, a wardrobe, and a desk in the room.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 방에는 침대 하나, 옷장 하나, 책상 하나가 있습니다.

drill_examples.text: 廚房裡有冰箱、洗衣機和熱水器。
drill_examples.pinyin: Chúfáng lǐ yǒu bīngxiāng, xǐyījī hé rèshuǐqì.
drill_examples.translation_en: The kitchen has a refrigerator, a washing machine, and a water heater.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 부엌에는 냉장고, 세탁기, 온수기가 있습니다.

drill_examples.text: 客廳有沙發、桌子和電視。
drill_examples.pinyin: Kètīng yǒu shāfā, zhuōzi hé diànshì.
drill_examples.translation_en: The living room has a sofa, a table, and a TV.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 거실에는 소파, 테이블, 텔레비전이 있습니다.

drill_examples.text: 浴室有馬桶、水槽和一個大窗戶。
drill_examples.pinyin: Yùshì yǒu mǎtǒng, shuǐcáo hé yī ge dà chuānghù.
drill_examples.translation_en: The bathroom has a toilet, a sink, and a big window.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 욕실에는 변기, 세면대, 큰 창문이 있습니다.

drill_examples.text: 陽台有兩張椅子和一盞燈。
drill_examples.pinyin: Yángtái yǒu liǎng zhāng yǐzi hé yī zhǎn dēng.
drill_examples.translation_en: The balcony has two chairs and a lamp.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 발코니에는 의자 두 개와 등 하나가 있습니다.

drill_examples.text: 這個房間沒有冷氣，但是有電風扇。
drill_examples.pinyin: Zhè ge fángjiān méiyǒu lěngqì, dànshì yǒu diànfēngshàn.
drill_examples.translation_en: This room doesn't have air conditioning, but it has an electric fan.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 이 방에는 에어컨이 없지만 선풍기가 있습니다.

drill_examples.text: 公寓的門前面有一個小陽台。
drill_examples.pinyin: Gōngyù de mén qiánmiàn yǒu yī ge xiǎo yángtái.
drill_examples.translation_en: There is a small balcony in front of the apartment door.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 아파트 문 앞에 작은 발코니가 있습니다.

negative_examples.error: Wrong word order: facility should come after 有, not before.

incorrect_examples.error: Using 一個 is unnecessary for general existence; just say 有冷氣.

transformation_drills: Change '房間有冷氣' to negative: '房間沒有冷氣'
Change '客廳有沙發' to question: '客廳有沙發嗎?'
production_drills: Describe your own room using 有: '我的房間有床和桌子。'
Ask a partner: '你的公寓有什麼？'
common_error_patterns: Putting facility before 有
Adding unnecessary measure words

repair_feedback.word_order: Remember: room first, then 有, then facility.
repair_feedback.politeness: This structure is neutral; no special politeness needed.
repair_feedback.vocabulary: Use facility words like 冷氣, 冰箱, 網路.

explanation_en: Use 在 to state where a facility is located within a room or apartment.
title: 在 for Location of Facility
meaning: The [facility] is at [location].
when_to_use: Pointing out where a specific facility is
Describing the layout of a room
when_not_to_use: When stating existence of a facility (use 有 instead)
When talking about people's location
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, when showing an apartment, you might say '冷氣在客廳' or '洗衣機在陽台'.
word_order_notes: Facility comes first, then 在, then the location. This is opposite to English word order.
english_contrast: English says 'The air conditioner is in the living room.' Chinese says '冷氣在客廳' (facility + 在 + location).
korean_contrast: Korean uses '에 있다' after location. Chinese puts location after 在.

correct_examples.text: 公寓在學校附近。
correct_examples.pinyin: Gōngyù zài xuéxiào fùjìn.
correct_examples.translation_en: The apartment is near the school.

correct_examples.text: 客廳在廚房的前面。
correct_examples.pinyin: Kètīng zài chúfáng de qiánmiàn.
correct_examples.translation_en: The living room is in front of the kitchen.

correct_examples.text: 浴室在房間的旁邊。
correct_examples.pinyin: Yùshì zài fángjiān de pángbiān.
correct_examples.translation_en: The bathroom is next to the room.

correct_examples.text: 陽台在客廳的外面。
correct_examples.pinyin: Yángtái zài kètīng de wàimiàn.
correct_examples.translation_en: The balcony is outside the living room.

correct_examples.text: 冰箱在廚房裡面。
correct_examples.pinyin: Bīngxiāng zài chúfáng lǐmiàn.
correct_examples.translation_en: The refrigerator is inside the kitchen.

correct_examples.text: 洗衣機在浴室裡面。
correct_examples.pinyin: Xǐyījī zài yùshì lǐmiàn.
correct_examples.translation_en: The washing machine is inside the bathroom.

correct_examples.text: 書桌在窗戶的旁邊。
correct_examples.pinyin: Shūzhuō zài chuānghù de pángbiān.
correct_examples.translation_en: The desk is next to the window.

correct_examples.text: 沙發在客廳的中間。
correct_examples.pinyin: Shāfā zài kètīng de zhōngjiān.
correct_examples.translation_en: The sofa is in the middle of the living room.

drill_examples.text: 公寓在學校附近。
drill_examples.pinyin: Gōngyù zài xuéxiào fùjìn.
drill_examples.translation_en: The apartment is near the school.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 아파트가 학교 근처에 있습니다.

drill_examples.text: 客廳在廚房的前面。
drill_examples.pinyin: Kètīng zài chúfáng de qiánmiàn.
drill_examples.translation_en: The living room is in front of the kitchen.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 거실이 주방 앞에 있습니다.

drill_examples.text: 浴室在房間的旁邊。
drill_examples.pinyin: Yùshì zài fángjiān de pángbiān.
drill_examples.translation_en: The bathroom is next to the room.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 욕실이 방 옆에 있습니다.

drill_examples.text: 陽台在客廳的外面。
drill_examples.pinyin: Yángtái zài kètīng de wàimiàn.
drill_examples.translation_en: The balcony is outside the living room.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 발코니가 거실 밖에 있습니다.

drill_examples.text: 冰箱在廚房裡面。
drill_examples.pinyin: Bīngxiāng zài chúfáng lǐmiàn.
drill_examples.translation_en: The refrigerator is inside the kitchen.

drill_examples.text: 洗衣機在浴室裡面。
drill_examples.pinyin: Xǐyījī zài yùshì lǐmiàn.
drill_examples.translation_en: The washing machine is inside the bathroom.
drill_examples.translation_ko: 세탁기가 욕실 안에 있습니다.

drill_examples.text: 書桌在窗戶的旁邊。
drill_examples.pinyin: Shūzhuō zài chuānghù de pángbiān.
drill_examples.translation_en: The desk is next to the window.
